elements.sent-2411-001: Language model post-training is applied to refine behaviors and unlock new skills across a wide range of recent language models, but open recipes for applying these techniques lag behind proprietary ones.
elements.sent-2411-002: The underlying training data and recipes for post-training are simultaneously the most important pieces of the puzzle and the portion with the least transparency.
elements.sent-2411-003: To bridge this gap, we introduce \modelname~3, a family of fully-open state-of-the-art post-trained models, alongside its data, code, and training recipes, serving as a comprehensive guide for modern post-training techniques.
elements.sent-2411-004: The training algorithms for our models include supervised finetuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and a novel method we call Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR).
elements.sent-2411-005: With \modelname~3, we build a multi-task evaluation scheme for post-training with development and unseen evaluations, standard benchmark implementations, and substantial decontamination of existing open datasets on said benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-006: We conclude with analysis and discussion of training methods that did not reliably improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: The \modelname~3 release includes model weights, a demo, and the complete recipe --- datasets for diverse core skills, a robust toolkit for data curation and evaluation, the training code and infrastructure, and, most importantly, a detailed report for reproducing and further adapting the \modelname~3 approach to more domains.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_images/tulu3_figure1_v2.pdf} \caption{An overview of the \modelname~3 recipe. This includes: data curation targeting general and target capabilities, training strategies and a standardized evaluation suite for development and final evaluation stage. } \label{fig:tulu3_recipe_overview}
elements.sent-2411-008: Post-training --- the collection of techniques including instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and other types of finetuning --- has become a crucial step in building frontier language models~\citep{OpenAI2024, Anthropic2024 , yet developments to these techniques are frequently not accompanied by open resources and recipes.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Fully open source counterparts (e.g.,~\modelname~2~\citep{ivison2023camels and Zephyr-$\beta$~\citep{tunstall2023zephyr ) often rely on simpler-to-implement and cheaper pipelines and have become outdated on many metrics.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: To close the gap between open and closed post training, we introduce {\bf \modelname~3 , a family of open state-of-the-art post-trained models, alongside all of the data, training recipes, code, infrastructure, and evaluation framework.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Integrating partial details from proprietary methods with novel techniques and established academic research, \modelname~3 pushes the boundaries of research in post-training.
elements.sent-2411-012: The advancements of \modelname~3 are attributed to \modelname~3 Data, new permissively licensed training datasets targeting core skills, \evalname, evaluation suite and tools to establish clear performance goals and guide improvement through training stages, and \modelname~3 Recipe, an advanced multi-stage training pipeline incorporating new algorithmic advancements in reinforcement learning, cutting-edge infrastructure, and rigorous experimentation to optimize data mixes, methods, and parameters across various training stages.
elements.sent-2411-013: In order to build \modelname~3, we identify a set of core skills to improve after training (e.g., reasoning, math, coding, safety, precise instruction following, knowledge recall, etc.) and build an evaluation framework to establish clear performance goals and guide model improvement over a selection of development and unseen tasks.
elements.sent-2411-014: The \modelname~3 training recipe involves multiple stages, with each stage building upon the previous model and focusing on different types of data --- namely, {\it prompt-completion instances for supervised finetuning, {\it preferences for preference tuning, or {\it verifiable rewards for reinforcement learning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Our methodology facilitates identifying skill deficiencies and refining the data mix, methods and parameters, ensuring a balanced performance of core skills across the training process.
elements.sent-2411-016: Through rigorous, principled experimentation, we determine the best data mix for supervised finetuning, resulting in the \modelname~3~SFT checkpoint.
elements.sent-2411-017: Leveraging recent advances in preference tuning, we then train a model over carefully curated {\it on-policy preference data from comparing \modelname~3~SFT completions against outputs from other language models.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, we introduce a new final finetuning stage -- Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) - which employs a novel RL objective tailored to enhance specific skills with verifiable answers, such as mathematics and precise instruction following.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our best performing recipe yields \modelname~3 models that outperform the state-of-the-art post-trained open-weight models of the same size such as Llama~3.1~Instruct~\citep{dubey2024llama , Qwen2.5~Instruct~\citep{qwen2.5 , or Mistral-Instruct~\citep{mistral2024ministraux , and at the large 70B size \modelname matches the offerings of closed providers such as Claude 3.5 Haiku and GPT-4o~mini.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In summary, \modelname~3 represents a family of state-of-the-art open language models, featuring a modern post-training framework with fully open-source data \modelname~3~Data, evaluation~\evalname, training code \modelname~3~Code and development recipes \modelname~3~Recipe.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here are a few key contributions from the development of \modelname:.
elements.sent-2411-022: Extensive guidance and tooling for evaluation, decontamination, and recipe design,.
elements.sent-2411-024: Scaling preference data with on-policy generations,.
elements.sent-2411-025: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, an RL-based method that only gets a reward if the model's completions are verified to be correct,.
elements.sent-2411-026: Advanced infrastructure, details, and code to facilitate the successful implementation of large models.

elements.sent-2411-027: The result of our work is completely open pipelines for finetuning language models.
elements.sent-2411-028: We release final models trained on Llama 3.1 base versions~\citep{dubey2024llama , with intermediate checkpoints, training data, training code, and evaluation code (a full list of artifacts released is available in Table~\ref{tab:artifacts ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: With all the released resources, others can take open base models and finetune them to high-performance on any task of interest -- laying the foundation of post-training research within complex, multi-objective and multi-stage training regimes.
elements.sent-2411-030: Early work in language model post-training followed a standard recipe pioneered by models like InstructGPT~\citep{ouyang2022training , consisting of instruction-tuning followed by preference finetuning (PreFT)~\citep{stiennon2020learning, nakano2021webgpt, askell2021general, ouyang2022training .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Since then, the sophistication and complexity of post-training approaches have continued to increase, moving towards multiple rounds of training, human data plus synthetic data, and multiple training algorithms and objectives~\citep{touvron2023llama, dubey2024llama, gunter2024apple .}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, most successful post-training models offer limited information about their training data, code, or recipes.\footnote{On LMSYS's ChatBotArena, no model in the top 50 (as of November 20th, 2024) has released its post-training data~\citep{chiang2024chatbot .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Open post-training research, such as \modelname~2~\citep{ivison2023camels and Zephyr-$\beta$~\citep{tunstall2023zephyr , show strong results in some benchmarks and on chat evaluations such as AlpacaEval or Arena-Hard~\citep{arenahard2024 , but still lag behind in core capabilities such as MATH~\citep{hendrycksmath2021 , IFEval~\citep{zhou2023instructionfollowingevaluationlargelanguage and GSM8K~\citep{cobbe2021gsm8k .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: With \modelname~3, we hope to uncover which paths for the open-source community will lead to success and which do not (by reporting negative results).
elements.sent-2411-035: It is a complex training process that integrates partial details from proprietary methods with novel techniques and established academic research.
elements.sent-2411-036: The key factors in the success of mdelname~3 are rooted in careful data curation, rigorous experimentation and evaluation, innovative methodologies, and improved training infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-037: We followed systematic guidelines by scientifically evaluating this process through creating development and test sets for evaluation, and conduct careful decontamination of publicly available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-038: By openly sharing our data, recipe and findings, we aim to empower the community to explore new and innovative post-training approaches.
elements.sent-2411-039: We list the extensive artifacts and tools released in Table~\ref{tab:artifacts .}

elements.sent-2411-040: Core Skills The \modelname~3 effort began with identifying key areas where open post-training recipes often fall behind and that are desirable capabilities for generalist language models.
elements.sent-2411-041: Table~\ref{tab:eval_suites outlines the core capabilities we aim to enhance and the evaluation benchmarks selected to cover these skills.}
elements.sent-2411-042: With \modelname~3, we focus on core skills of knowledge recall, reasoning, mathematics, coding, instruction following, general chat, and safety.
elements.sent-2411-043: We curate and collect \modelname~3 Data to target these core skills by sourcing from public data and synthetically curating data.
elements.sent-2411-044: We use various data formats at different stages of training.
elements.sent-2411-045: Table \ref{tab:sft_summary outlines the collection of datasets used to train our model, and further details are provided in Section \autoref{sec:prompts .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: A key factor in the success of our post-training approach is establishing clear performance goals and evaluation tools to guide improvement through these stages.
elements.sent-2411-047: With \evalname, we release a unified standardized evaluation suite and a toolkit to guide the development and assessing final models and decontaminating training data against evaluation benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-048: Our framework consists of an open evaluation toolkit for reproducible evaluations (Section~\ref{sec:olmes ), a suite for evaluating core skills in instruction-tuned models with separate development (Section~\ref{sec:dev_suite ) and held-out evaluations (Section~\ref{sec:unseen_suite ), and a set of recommended settings for evaluating on our evaluation suite based on our experiments with various models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Both splits cover all identified skills.
elements.sent-2411-050: Crucially, we did not examine scores on our unseen set when developing our models, allowing us to observe how much we may have overfit to particular evaluations in our decisions around data mixtures, algorithms, and hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-052: We provide further details on our evaluations in Section~\ref{sec:evaluation and in Table~\ref{tab:test_settings .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We publicly release our evaluation suite at \url{https://github.com/allenai/olmes .}

elements.sent-2411-054: In this section, we provide an overview of the \modelname~3 recipe to obtain a state-of-the-art post-trained model.
elements.sent-2411-055: We produce \modelname~3 models through a four-stage post-training recipe on top of pretrained language models (see \autoref{fig:tulu3_recipe_overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: The \modelname~3 Recipe is an advanced multi-stage training pipeline incorporating new algorithmic advancements in reinforcement learning, cutting-edge infrastructure, and rigorous experimentation to curate data and optimize data mixes, methods, and parameters across various training stages.
elements.sent-2411-057: Throughout all stages, we measure model performance using a carefully-chosen evaluation suite.
elements.sent-2411-059: We create new synthetic prompts or, when available, source prompts from existing datasets to target specific capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-060: We ensure prompts are not contaminated with our evaluation suite, \evalname.
elements.sent-2411-061: With thorough experimentation, the final SFT data and training hyperparameters are determined to enhance target core skills without significantly impacting the performance of others, guided by our evaluation framework.
elements.sent-2411-062: As in the SFT stage, we identify the best preference data mix through thorough experimentation, uncovering what formats of data, methods, or hyperparameters lead to improvements.
elements.sent-2411-063: We select tasks with verifiable outcomes, such as mathematical problem-solving, and only provide rewards when the model's generations are verified to be correct.
elements.sent-2411-064: We then use RL to train on these rewards.
elements.sent-2411-065: The key contributions of our \modelname~3 pipeline lie in improved data, methods, infrastructure, and rigorous evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-066: Key elements of our pipeline include:.
elements.sent-2411-067: Data Quality, Provenance, and Scale (\S\ref{sec:prompts ) We obtain prompts by carefully surveying available open-source datasets, analyzing their provenance, and decontaminating them, as well as curating synthetic prompts that target core skills.}
elements.sent-2411-068: To ensure effectiveness, we conduct thorough experiments to study their impact on our development evaluation suite.
elements.sent-2411-069: We find targeted prompts to be influential to improve core skills, while real-world queries, e.g., WildChat~\citep{zhao2024wildchat , are important to improve general chat capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Using the \evalname decontamination tool, we ensure prompts are not contaminated against our evaluation suite.\footnote{ We observe a non-trivial amount of contamination in a few open datasets with popular evaluation benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Creating a Multi-Skill SFT Dataset (\S\ref{sec:sft_data ) The distribution of the prompts in the ``general'' and ``skill-specific'' categories was refined by several rounds of supervised finetuning on various data mixtures.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For example, to improve mathematical reasoning, we first establish an upper bound in our evaluation suite by creating math-specialized models, then mix data to bring the general models closer to this upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-074: Curating an On-Policy Preference Dataset (\S\ref{sec:pref_pipeline_main ) We develop an on-policy data curation pipeline to scale our preference dataset generation.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Concretely, we generate completions from \modelname~3-SFT and other models for given prompts, and obtain preference labels through their pairwise comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-076: Our approach extends and improves the off-policy preference data generation method by~\citet{cui2023ultrafeedback .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Careful multi-skill selection of preference data yields 354,192 instances for preference tuning demonstrating significant improvements in a range of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-078: Preference Tuning Algorithm Design (\S\ref{sec:preference_tuning_recipe ) We experiment with several preference tuning algorithms and observe improved performance in using length-normalized Direct Preference Optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-079: We prioritized simplicity and efficiency in experimentation and used length-normalized DPO throughout the development process and training our final models, in lieu of more costly investigations into PPO-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-080: Skill-Specific RL with Verifiable Rewards (\S\ref{sec:rlvr ) We adapt a new approach, leveraging a standard reinforcement-learning paradigm to target skills that can be evaluated against a ground-truth outcome (e.g., Math).}
elements.sent-2411-081: We refer to this algorithm as Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR); it obtains a constant reward value if a completion is successful.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our results show that RLVR can improve GSM8K, MATH, and IFEval performance.
elements.sent-2411-083: Training Infrastructure for Reinforcement Learning (\S\ref{subsec:rl_infra ): We implemented an asynchronous RL setup: we run LLM inference efficiently via vLLM while the learners perform gradient updates concurrently.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Our RL codebase is also highly scalable and can train 70B RLVR policy models.
elements.sent-2411-085: Evaluation Framework: \evalname (\S\ref{sec:evaluation ) In addition to evaluating the final models, our evaluation framework is an open evaluation toolkit designed to guide the development progress through carefully selected evaluation suite and tools for decontamination.}
elements.sent-2411-086: When reporting scores throughout this work, we use the metrics identified in Table~\ref{tab:eval_suites ; higher is always better.}
elements.sent-2411-087: When computing overall performance, we simply average scores across all evaluations, treating each evaluation equally.
elements.sent-2411-088: Compared to closed models, \modelname~3 70B even surpasses closed models such as GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125 or GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18, while approaching the performance of Claude 3.5 Haiku 20241022.
elements.sent-2411-089: The summary of \modelname~3 trained on Llama 3 at 8 and 70 billion parameters versus the leading models in their sizes classes is shown in Table~\ref{tab:overview .}
elements.sent-2411-090: A per training stage breakdown of performance is shown for the 8B version in Table~\ref{tab:8b_results and for 70B in Table~\ref{tab:70b_results .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: With our models trained from raw pretrained base models, we compare to instruct models trained on the same base models (e.g. Nous Hermes 3), instruct models on similar sized, but different base versions (e.g. Ministral 8B or Qwen 2.5 Instruct), and other finetuning recipes trained on an instruct version (e.g. Nemotron Llama 3.1).
elements.sent-2411-092: At 70B, we compare to and surpass Llama 3.1 70B Instruct, Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct~\citep{qwen2.5 , Nous Hermes 3 70B~\citep{teknium2024hermes (trained on Llama 3.1 70B), and Nemotron Llama 3.1 70B~\citep{wang2024helpsteer2p (trained on Llama 3.1 70B Instruct).}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: At 8B, we compare to and surpass Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Gemma 2 9B Instruct~\citep{team2024gemma , Nous Hermes 3 8B (trained on Llama 3.1 8B), Qwen 72.5B Instruct, and Ministral 8B Instruct 2410.}
elements.sent-2411-094: We release all artifacts associated with the \modelname~3 training recipe -- including SFT, DPO, and RL model checkpoints, along with new SFT and DPO datasets.
elements.sent-2411-095: A summary of the artifacts released with \modelname~3 is included in Table~\ref{tab:artifacts .}

elements.sent-2411-096: Prompts represent the diverse ways users may interact with models and serve as the essential component for all post-training stages.
elements.sent-2411-097: We curate an extensive collection of million prompts as the starting point of \modelname~3 post-training recipe.
elements.sent-2411-098: Data selected for next stages of training are selected from these prompts.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this section, we describe our prompt curation process and the decontamination effort to ensure that our evaluations are not leaked in these prompts.
elements.sent-2411-100: In the following sections, we describe how prompts are used for supervised finetuning \S\ref{sec:sft and preference tuning \S\ref{sec:preft .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: To target the desired core skills, we curate a {\it diverse and {\it high quality set of prompts from publicly available datasets with clear provenance and synthetically generate prompts to fill any gaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Since the release of our \modelname~2, the community has witnessed a large body of work creating datasets for post-training, in terms of both supervised finetuning and preference tuning.
elements.sent-2411-103: We start this process with a broad survey of public datasets, including those annotated by dedicated workers, sourced from real users, and synthesized with models.
elements.sent-2411-104: We then manually review each individual dataset, and pick those with the following considerations.
elements.sent-2411-105: The diversity of training data is critical for eliciting models' generalization, avoiding model forgetting, and making models robust to uncommon inputs~\citep{wang2022super, chung2024scaling, zhou2024lima .}
elements.sent-2411-106: We pick datasets that can promote diversity, including: WildChat~\citep{zhao2024wildchat , which is a large source of real-user interaction with models; Open Assistant~\citep{kopf2024openassistant , which is created by volunteer workers for general chatting; No Robots~\citep{no_robots , which is annotated by expert workers for a broad range of open-ended categories; and FLAN v2~\citep{longpre2023flan , which is a big compilation of classical NLP tasks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: We also include a decontaminated subset of UltraFeedback~\citep{cui2023ultrafeedback , which is a composition of several datasets (FalseQA~\citep{hu2023won , UltraChat~\citep{ding2023enhancing , Evol-Instruct~\citep{xu2023wizardlm , FLAN v2~\citep{longpre2023flan ) and has shown strong performance for general preference tuning in early studies~\citep{tunstall2023zephyr,ivison2024unpacking .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We especially consider enhancing several capabilities that can power common use cases and our special needs.
elements.sent-2411-109: As shown in our earlier study~\citep{wang2023far , some capabilities, such as complex reasoning, coding, and precise instruction following, benefit from mixing in additional data.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, we include the following datasets: OpenMathInstruct~\citep{toshniwal2024openmathinstruct and NuminaMath~\citep{numina_math_7b for math reasoning, Evol-CodeAlpaca for coding, a subset of Daring-Anteater~\citep{wang2024helpsteer2 for precise instruction following, Aya~\citep{singh2024aya for multilinguality, SciRIFF~\citep{wadden2024sciriff for scientific literature understanding, and TableGPT~\citep{zha2023tablegpt for processing table-related tasks.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: We have also considered other datasets for domains with plenty of studies (e.g., math), but they either did not bring additional benefits in our early supervised finetuning experiments or have license issues.
elements.sent-2411-112: When sourcing prompts, we take careful consideration of the licenses of the original datasets and only allow those with clear and correct licenses.
elements.sent-2411-113: Since many publicly released datasets are compositions of other datasets, we have to manually track the provenance of subsets to verify their licenses and remove those that have issues.
elements.sent-2411-114: Specifically, the ShareGPT dataset\footnote{ShareGPT data was initially used to build the Vicuna model~\citep{vicuna2023 , but the exact dataset has not been released.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Later work mainly used a community reproduced version at \url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/anon8231489123/ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered/ .}
elements.sent-2411-116: We also removed the relevant subset from UltraFeedback and decided not to use Helpsteer2 \citep{wang2024helpsteer2 due to the use of ShareGPT in their prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-117: All the datasets included in our final curation have clear licenses, and we have a smaller commercial-friendly set and a larger non-commercial set based on whether the licenses allow commercial use.
elements.sent-2411-118: To address the growing need for diverse and skill-specific datasets, we incorporate synthetic data generation as a complementary approach.
elements.sent-2411-119: Synthetic data generation has gained traction as a promising alternative to human-written data due to being easier to obtain, customizable for different purposes, and reflecting the vast knowledge of the underlying models~\citep{dubey2024llama .}
elements.sent-2411-120: However, generating diverse and high-quality data at scale is non-trivial, as LMs are susceptible to falling into repetitive modes or patterns, referred to as ``mode collapse'' \citep{kazdan2024collapsethriveperilspromises .}
elements.sent-2411-121: To ensure diversity in generation, we follow the recent persona-driven methodology in \citet{chan2024scaling to generate synthetic data.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The key idea is to use different personas (e.g., ``A machine learning researcher focused on neural networks'') with a data synthesis prompt (e.g., ``create a coding problem'') to steer an LLM to synthesize data with corresponding perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-123: Specifically, we condition on $\sim$250K personas from Persona Hub \citep{chan2024scaling to generate prompts targeting specific skills such as precise instruction following, math and coding.}
elements.sent-2411-124: We detail our procedure for each select skill below.
elements.sent-2411-125: Prompts used to generate these instructions can be found in Appendix~\ref{appdx:sft-prompts .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, we build upon our previous efforts in~\cite{brahman2024art,han2024wildguard,wildteaming2024 , to generate noncompliance and safety data.. Precise Instruction Following.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Precise instruction following is the ability to follow verifiable instructions in natural language, such as ``write 3 paragraphs,'' that can be automatically verified with heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-128: We use our persona-driven approach to synthetically generate verifiable instructions covering 25 different constraint types defined in IFEval benchmark \citep{zhou2023instructionfollowingevaluationlargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-129: More concretely, we start by manually writing 1-2 example instructions per constraint (e.g., number of words), resulting in total of 33 verifiable instructions which we used as seed prompts.
elements.sent-2411-130: We then generate new instructions using GPT-4o~\citep{openai2024gpt4o}\footnote{We use GPT-4o-2024-08-06 for all our persona-driven data synthesis, unless otherwise stated.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Figures \ref{fig:persona-prompt-sft-if and \ref{fig:persona-prompt-if-res show the exact prompts used to generate the instruction and its corresponding response, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: In total, we collected 29,980 verifiable instruction-response pairs which we call If-Persona-Sft.. Math and Coding.
elements.sent-2411-133: We follow a similar persona-driven approach to synthetically generate diverse math word and coding problems.
elements.sent-2411-134: Math problems include those that require advanced mathematical skills as well as grade school problems.
elements.sent-2411-135: For coding, we generate python programming questions that are solvable by entry- to medium-level programmers.
elements.sent-2411-136: Unlike precise instruction following, we zero-shot prompt GPT-4o to generate problems that are unique and specific to a given persona input.
elements.sent-2411-137: Having generated the problems, we then generate multi-step math solutions using GPT-4o, and python programs using claude-3-5-sonnet.
elements.sent-2411-138: Exact prompts used to generate problems and solutions are provided in Figures \ref{fig:persona-prompt-sft-math , \ref{fig:persona-prompt-sft-code , \ref{fig:persona-prompt-sft-math-res , and \ref{fig:persona-prompt-sft-code-res , respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: In total, we collected $\sim$220K and 35K instances for math reasoning and coding.. Noncompliance and Safety.
elements.sent-2411-140: As we enhance models’ capabilities to assist users effectively, it is crucial to ensure they can reliability reject unsafe and appropriately handle nuanced and out of scope queries.
elements.sent-2411-141: To support this, we curate a set of noncompliance queries \citep{brahman2024art that the model ought to not comply with, alongside safety-related direct and adversarial prompts \citep{han2024wildguard,wildteaming2024 covering both benign and harmful scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Our noncompliance and safety prompts are either curated from existing datasets \citep{zhang-choi-2021-situatedqa, zhao2024wildchat or synthetically generated from GPT model family.}
elements.sent-2411-143: More specifically, our noncompliance prompts are obtained based on our contextual noncompliance taxonomy from \citet{brahman2024art , spanning multiple categories including incomplete, unsupported, indeterminate, and humanizing requests (in addition to unsafe requests).}
elements.sent-2411-144: Our safety-related prompts are carefully selected among synthetic adversarial prompts, synthetic vanilla (direct) requests, real-world user-LLM interactions (In-The-Wild), and curated annotator-written examples to maximize coverage, diversity, and balance.
elements.figure-2411-008: \setlength\tabcolsep{5pt} \begin{tabular}{L{73pt}L{48pt}L{275pt}C{20pt}} Dataset Eval. \textbf{ {\huggingface} Link} \% $\downarrow$ \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} Evol CodeAlpaca HumanEval {Orig:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{ise-uiuc/Magicoder-Evol-Instruct-110K} \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} {New:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/evol_codealpaca_heval_decontaminated} \multirow{-2}{*}{3.5} WildChat GPT-4 Safety {Orig:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/WildChat-1M-Full} {(GPT-4 instances only)} \multirow{2}{*}{5.4} {New:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/wildchat_gpt4_converted_safety_decontaminated} \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} WildJailbreak Safety {Orig:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/wildjailbreak} \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} {New:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/wildjailbreak_safety_decontaminated} \multirow{-2}{*}{0.7} WildGuardmix Safety {Orig:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/wildguardmix} \multirow{2}{*}{1.1} {New:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/wildguardmixtrain_safety_decontaminated} \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} NuminaMath-TIR MATH {Orig:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{AI-MO/NuminaMath-TIR} \rowcolor{ai2offwhite} {New:} \huggingfacedatasettinynologo{allenai/numinamath_tir_math_decontaminated} \multirow{-2}{*}{11.3} \end{tabular} \caption{Decontaminated datasets. \% is the percent of the dataset removed.} \label{tab:decontamdatasets}
elements.sent-2411-145: One important consideration when curating our training mix was possible overlap between training and evaluation sets.
elements.sent-2411-146: We quantify such overlap as follows and remove instances from our training mix as needed in order to prevent test set contamination.. Matching Method.
elements.sent-2411-147: We experimented with full-string, n-gram, and embedding-based matching and found that n-gram matching yielded the most useful results --- while embedding-based methods can in principle identify non-trivial contamination like that due to paraphrasing~\citep{yang2023rethinking , we found it difficult to distinguish mere distributional similarity from actual paraphrasing.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Moreover, partial surface-level overlap using n-gram matching successfully identified cases of contamination where the instances were trivially different, e.g., a math problem where only the numbers differ.. Identifying Matching Instances.
elements.sent-2411-149: Since completions in training datasets are often regenerated using language models, we chose to compute overlap in the prompts alone (or more generally user turns in multi-turn dialogues).
elements.sent-2411-150: We used 8-gram matching for our contamination checks following~\citep{dubey2024llama,singh2024evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-151: For each token in a test instance, we consider it to match a token in a train instance if the two instances share an 8-gram containing that token, and we consider the test instance itself to have significant overlap with a train instance if more than 50\% of the test tokens have 8-gram matches with the same training instance.. Decontamination.
elements.sent-2411-152: We consider a training set to be contaminated if any number of its instances overlap with more than 2\% of the instances in any of the evaluations in our development and unseen suites.
elements.sent-2411-153: We remove all the training sets that were contaminated with our unseen evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-154: For training sets that were contaminated with our development evaluations, we removed the entire dataset if doing so did not significantly impact the performance of the resulting model; otherwise, we removed the specific instances that match any test instance.
elements.sent-2411-155: The list of datasets we decontaminated and the versions we released with overlapping samples removed is shown in Table~\ref{tab:decontamdatasets .}
elements.sent-2411-156: The full list of public datasets that we found to be significantly contaminated with our evaluation sets can be found in Table~\ref{tab:contaminated_datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Adapting pretrained base models to various tasks and user requests often relies on supervised finetuning (SFT), also known as instruction finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-158: A key challenge in this process is balancing the proportions of mixed training datasets representing diverse skills.
elements.sent-2411-159: For \modelname~3, we conducted data mixture ablations and explored model merging techniques to develop an SFT training procedure that well balances performance across the core skills we prioritized.
elements.sent-2411-160: The following sections detail our experiments and findings.

Compare this distribution to previous open SFT training datasets in Fig.~\ref{fig:sft-distribution-other}. Datasets with the most instances are on the bottom of the histogram. } \label{fig:sft-distribution}
elements.sent-2411-161: To create our SFT mix, we collect or create responses for prompts described in Section~\ref{sec:prompts in two ways: filtering existing responses, and creating new responses.}
elements.sent-2411-162: For prompts with existing responses, we generally keep the original response if it was written by a human or a frontier model, like GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-163: For large datasets with subsets from frontier models (e.g. WildChat), we use the subset from the best models.
elements.sent-2411-164: We additionally filter empty responses and responses that contain information about models or their developers.
elements.sent-2411-165: If a set of prompts did not have responses, like our Persona prompts, or if the original responses were from a weaker model (e.g. WildGuardMix), we generate new responses using GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-166: We also hand-wrote responses to our hardcoded prompts.
elements.sent-2411-167: To develop our SFT mix, we first identified the skills that were lagging behind state of the art models using Llama 3.1 trained on \modelname~2\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/allenai/llama-3.1-tulu-2-8b as our baseline.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Targeting each of these skills in isolation, we collected high quality publicly available datasets and created synthetic datasets, as described in Section~\ref{sec:synth_data_gen , and also removed some datasets that we identified to be of relatively lower quality compared to other more recent datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-169: To design our final SFT mix, we first built skill-specific data mixtures and models, keeping the mixtures that led to the best performance on individual skills, ignoring other evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-170: This was done to approximate the upper bound for each evaluation given our setup.
elements.sent-2411-171: We then combined these mixtures to create our initial \modelname~3 preview mix.
elements.sent-2411-172: We then continued to iterate on the mixture by adding or removing datasets to improve lagging skills, decontaminating against our evaluations and downsampling particularly large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-173: We show the performance of major preview versions throughout development in Figure~\ref{fig:tulu_dev_versions .}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figures_images/tulu_version_bars.pdf} { \cblock{247}{200}{226}~\modelname~2 \quad \cblock{231}{238}{238}~Intermediate Mix 1 \quad \cblock{206}{220}{221}~Intermediate Mix 2 \quad \cblock{159}{185}{187}~Intermediate Mix 3 \cblock{136}{168}{171}~Intermediate Mix 4 \quad \cblock{110}{151}{154}~Intermediate Mix 5 \quad \cblock{240}{82}{156}~\modelname~3 } \captionof{figure}{Average and selected skill-specific performance from training Llama 3.1 8B on our initial \modelname~2 SFT mix, and our intermediate and final \modelname~3 SFT mixes. Intermediate mixes 1, 2, and 3 were the result of adding new datasets to improve performance. Intermediate mixes 4 and 5 were the result of running multiple rounds of decontamination, causing small drops in performance.} \label{fig:tulu_dev_versions}
elements.sent-2411-174: In Table~\ref{tab:sft-results , we compare our final \tuluthreesmallsft and \tuluthreelargesft models against other SFT-only models trained on Llama 3 8B or 70B.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Our new SFT mix shows substantial improvements over the \modelname~2 mix at both model sizes, and is better on average the other competitive 8B SFT models.

elements.sent-2411-176: We also ran a series of controlled experiments after developing our final SFT mix to explore the importance of different decisions made during data mixing and training.

elements.sent-2411-177: In our mix we also emphasized adding diverse chat data, mainly from WildChat.
elements.sent-2411-178: We show the impact of removing WildChat in Table~\ref{tab:sft-ablations , and we see that there is a small but noticeable degradation on most skills, most noticeably on Alpaca Eval, highlighting the importance of diverse real-world data.}
elements.sent-2411-179: We found that our safety SFT data was generally orthogonal to our other datasets.
elements.sent-2411-180: We report the effect of removing our safety-specific datasets in Table~\ref{tab:sft-ablations , and we see that most skills stayed roughly the same, except the safety average.}
elements.sent-2411-181: We also found that adding constrastive prompts, such as those in CoCoNot, were helpful for preventing our models from over-refusing safe prompts.
elements.sent-2411-182: Our new Persona datasets were built to target specific skills: mathematics, coding, and instruction following.
elements.sent-2411-183: In Table~\ref{tab:sft-ablations we show that performance on HumanEval(+), GSM8K, MATH, and IFEval drop after removing our Persona datasets, showing the value of creating diverse, skill-specific SFT datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-184: We also show the impact of targeting specific skills in Table~\ref{tab:sft-ablations , where we show the effect of removing all mathematics-specific data.}
elements.sent-2411-185: We see that both GSM8K and MATH drop significantly compared to our final mix, emphasizing the positive impact of focusing on specific skills during data collection.
elements.sent-2411-186: Using mathematical reasoning as an illustrative example, we show in Table~\ref{tab:sft-ablations the impact of our mathematics-specific data on both GSM8K and MATH.}
elements.sent-2411-187: We see that our mathematics-specific SFT data substantially improves both GSM8K and MATH, showing the value of the data included in the final mix.
elements.sent-2411-188: In Figure~\ref{fig:downsampling , we show the effect of taking stratified subsamples of our SFT mix.}
elements.sent-2411-189: We find that our models continue to improve on average as more SFT data is included, and we see large improvements on metrics like GSM8K as we increase the amount of data to the full mix.
elements.sent-2411-190: Interestingly, TruthfulQA performance actually drops as the amount of data in the mix increases.

elements.sent-2411-209: Early during training \modelname~3, we noticed a gap in performance between SFT models trained on our Open-Instruct framework and models trained in other settings such as on TPUs.\footnote{Relevant code: \url{https://github.com/hamishivi/EasyLM We found this issue was largely due to a (recently widely-reported) issue with loss aggregation inside Transformers~\citep{wolf-etal-2020-transformers : Averaging the loss across padding tokens without taking into account gradient accumulation or distributed training setups.}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: Here, we illustrate the issue with an example.
elements.sent-2411-211: Assume we have two samples in a batch, with $n_1$, $n_2$ non-padding tokens and $m_1$, $m_2$ padding tokens.
elements.sent-2411-212: If we pass both samples into the default Transformers forward pass at the same time, we get: \begin{equation}L = \frac{l_{n_1} + l_{n_2}}{n_1 + n_2}\end{equation} However, if we apply gradient accumulation, feeding in the two samples separately, computing loss, and then dividing, our loss is instead computed like: \begin{equation}L = \frac{\frac{l_{n_1}}{n_1} + \frac{l_{n_2}}{n_2}}{2}\end{equation} That is, in the second case we weight each example equally, while in the first we weight each token equally.
elements.sent-2411-213: As such, changing gradient accumulation can have large effects on performance due to effectively changing sample weightings, as reported by \citet{muennighoff2024generative .}
elements.sent-2411-214: A similar issue occurs in distributed training due to cross-device averaging.
elements.sent-2411-215: We refer to recent reports on this issue for a more in-depth explanation.\footnote{\url{https://unsloth.ai/blog/gradient , \url{https://muellerzr.github.io/blog/gradient_accumulation_part2.html To fix this issue, we opted generally to use a sum loss instead of averaging (`mean loss') when training.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: This removes the issue by simply removing the denominator from the above equations and requires an adjustment to learning rates.
elements.sent-2411-217: This effectively weights all tokens equally (which we found led to generally better performance for initial mixtures).
elements.sent-2411-218: We validated the perfomance of our setup by finetuning Llama 3.0 on the \modelname 2 SFT mixture using a variety of learning rates, epochs, and loss types as shown in Figures~\ref{fig:sft_lr_ablate and ~\ref{fig:sft_epoch_ablate .}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Ultimately, we found that using a sum loss with a learning rate of 5.00E-06 worked best.
elements.sent-2411-220: Surprisingly, we additionally found that training for longer did not yield further improvements, and so used 2 epochs for training.
elements.sent-2411-221: We find that a LR of 5e-6 with a sum loss works best.
elements.sent-2411-222: We find using 2 epochs works best.
elements.sent-2411-223: We explore multiple training algorithms, from Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its derivatives to reinforcement learning algorithms such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).
elements.sent-2411-224: In this section, we detail the problem formulation of learning from human preferences and our optimizers.
elements.sent-2411-225: Next, we explain how to convert our prompts into synthetic preference data from both on-policy (\modelname~3 suite) and off-policy models (other instruct models.
elements.sent-2411-226: We show how to create preference data for specific skills of interest and how we improve our models robustly with DPO.
elements.sent-2411-227: Prior work has established training on preference data as a crucial step for improving model performance on benchmarks simulating human or synthetic preferences~\citep{dubois2023alpacafarm, ivison2023camels, ivison2024unpacking .}
elements.sent-2411-228: The typical procedure is reinforcement learning from human or synthetic feedback ~\citep{ziegler2019fine,stiennon2020learning,ouyang2022training,bai2022constitutional .}
elements.sent-2411-229: In the standard setup, there is some preference dataset $\mathcal{D}$ consisting of prompts $x$ and two responses $y, y'$ per prompt.
elements.sent-2411-230: Some judges will choose one of $y, y'$ as their preferred response $y_c$, and label the other as a rejected response $y_r$.. Reward Model.
elements.sent-2411-231: Given the preference dataset, a reward model (RM) $r_\phi$ is trained with the following objective: \begin{equation}\max_{r_\phi} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y_c,y_r)\sim\mathcal{D}}[\log \sigma(r_\phi(x,y_c) - r_\phi(x,y_r))]\end{equation} where $\sigma$ is the logistic function.
elements.sent-2411-232: The RM objective maximizes the difference between the rewards, and this difference represents the log-likelihood that $y_c$ will be preferred over $y_r$~\citep{ouyang2022training .}
elements.sent-2411-233: This reward model can help train policy models to output contents preferred by the RM's judgments.
elements.sent-2411-234: There are a plethora of options for optimizing language models with access to preference data.
elements.sent-2411-235: Today, the two categories can be abstracted as reinforcement learning algorithms, which learn from an internal representation of value or reward, and direct alignment algorithms, which learn directly from the data.
elements.sent-2411-236: Prior work~\citep{ziegler2019fine,stiennon2020learning,ouyang2022training optimizes the policy $\pi_\theta$ with the following objective: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rlhf-objective} \max_{\pi_\theta} \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi_\theta(x)} \left[R(x, y)\right] = \left[ r_\phi(x,y) - \beta \text{KL}[\pi_\theta(y|x) \| \pi_\text{ref} (y|x)] \right]\end{equation} where $\pi_\text{ref}$ is the initial reference policy, and the $\beta$ coefficient helps control the Kullback-Lieber divergence (KL) divergence between the reference policy and the training policy.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Here, we explain PPO and DPO as representative examples.
elements.sent-2411-239: An approach to address the above objective is to use online reinforcement learning (RL) like PPO~\citep{schulman2017proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-240: In each training iteration of PPO, the policy needs to generate some samples, generate rewards using the RM on those samples, and maximize $R(x, y)$ using the PPO algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-241: As PPO training loops are complex, we refer the reader to \citet{ouyang2022training, ivison2024unpacking, huang2024thenimplementationdetails for more thorough descriptions of the setup and typical setups.}
elements.sent-2411-242: We provide more implementation details in Sec~\ref{sec:rl_recipe .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Another approach is offline preference tuning.
elements.sent-2411-245: DPO~\citep{rafailov2024direct can directly optimizes for the RLHF objective with the following equivalent objective: \begin{equation}\max_{\pi_\theta} \mathbb{E}_{y_c,y_r \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \log \sigma \left( \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_c | x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_c | x)} -\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_r | x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_r | x)} \right) \right] \label{eq:dpo_eq}\end{equation} DPO trains an implicit reward model and a policy model simultaneously, without needing to use a trained reward model, do policy generations, and get rewards from the RM.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\max_{\pi_\theta} E_{y_c,y_r \sim D} \left[ \log \sigma \left( \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_c | x)}{\pi_ref(y_c | x)} -\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_r | x)}{\pi_ref(y_r | x)} \right) \right] \label{eq:dpo_eq}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-246: Crucially, this allows offline preference finetuning, directly training a language model on preference pairs gathered from a variety of sources.
elements.sent-2411-247: Recently, much work has examined how to further improve the DPO objective, with a multitude of variants proposed~\citep[inter alia]{meng2024simpo, xu2024contrastivepreferenceoptimizationpushing, hong-etal-2024-orpo .}
elements.sent-2411-248: In this work, we explored two promising variants: {\bf SimPO ~\citep{meng2024simpo and {\bf length-normalized DPO}\footnote{As proposed in the original~\citet{rafailov2024direct , but was not yet well optimized to successful hyperparameters until~\citet{meng2024simpo .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: We find (in Section~\ref{sec:preference_tuning_recipe ) that length-normalized DPO works best, which uses the following objective: \begin{equation}\max_{\pi_\theta} \mathbb{E}_{y_c,y_r \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \log \sigma \left( \frac{\beta}{|y_c|} \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_c | x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_c | x)} -\frac{\beta}{|y_r|} \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_r | x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_r | x)} \right) \right]\end{equation} As seen, this is simply the DPO objective (Eq~\ref{eq:dpo_eq ), but with log-probabilities normalized for length, which intuitively aids with mitigating the length bias common in human and model preferences~\citep{singhal2024a .}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\max_{\pi_\theta} E_{y_c,y_r \sim D} \left[ \log \sigma \left( \frac{\beta}{|y_c|} \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_c | x)}{\pi_ref(y_c | x)} -\frac{\beta}{|y_r|} \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_r | x)}{\pi_ref(y_r | x)} \right) \right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-250: When developing \modelname 3, we opted to use length-normalized DPO for tuning our preference data mixtures and generation methods due to its relative simplicity and speed compared to approaches such as PPO.
elements.sent-2411-251: Our early experiments show the benefit of this pipeline in creating preference data, which leads to a high-quality, synthetic preference dataset (as observed by~\citet{ivison2024unpacking ).}
elements.sent-2411-252: Our data creation pipeline (shown in \autoref{fig:ufpp_pipeline ) consists of three stages: prompt selection, response generation from a pool of models, and preference annotation with LLM-as-a-judge to create (preferred, rejected) pairs.}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_images/ufpp_pipeline.pdf} \caption{ Pipeline for generating and scaling preference data that is based from Ultrafeedback \citep{cui2023ultrafeedback}. } \label{fig:ufpp_pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-254: The first step for preparing a dataset for preference finetuning is to select the prompts or user instructions to generate responses and obtain preferences for.
elements.sent-2411-255: Given the set of prompts in \autoref{tab:sft_summary , we curate our selection to include prompts used during SFT, and prompts that were subsampled from the same sources, yet unused, for SFT.}
elements.sent-2411-256: We also include prompts from other sources, such as a version of Ultrafeedback without TruthfulQA instances, or by adding new IF-constraints to a prompt.
elements.sent-2411-258: For a given prompt, we randomly sample four models from a model pool to generate responses.
elements.sent-2411-259: Our model selection is inspired by the Ultrafeedback pipeline which consists of open-source and proprietary models that vary across parameter size and model family.
elements.sent-2411-260: We update Ultrafeedback's model pool by using recent versions of some models (Llama 2 $\rightarrow$ Llama 3.1), adding best-performing models to increase the pool size, and replacing currently inaccessible models such as WizardLM with open-source alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-261: Finally, we also include on-policy data by sampling completions from the \modelname{ SFT model.}
elements.sent-2411-262: We approach this by adding a selection of prompts where one response is generated from the on-policy model, and the other response from the off-policy models.
elements.sent-2411-264: After generating four responses for each prompt, we use an LLM-as-a-judge \citep{zheng2023judging , specifically GPT-4o-2024-0806, to rate each response from 1 to 5 across four different aspects: helpfulness, instruction-following, honesty, and truthfulness.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Appendix \ref{appdx:pref-prompts shows the external models used to sample off-policy data and the prompt template for each aspect.}
elements.sent-2411-266: In order to obtain binary preferences for DPO, we obtain the mean of preference ratings similar to Argilla's binarization method\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/argilla/ultrafeedback-binarized-preferences/blob/main/README.md and take the highest-rated response as the chosen response and randomly sample from the responses with the lower mean as the rejected response.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: IF is short for Instruction Following.
elements.sent-2411-268: Most of the preference data mix ablations are run for the 8b model, We start with prompts used for SFT and generate on-policy and off-policy preference data, resulting in 96911 (off-policy) and 19444 (on-policy) preference instances.
elements.sent-2411-269: Given this preference base we ablate adding additional prompt sources to the mix and how these additions affect downstream evaluation performance, specifically targeting skills like precise instruction following, math and general chat performance on alpacaeval.
elements.sent-2411-270: Table~\ref{tab:prefmixing shows how the inclusion or exclusion of preference datasets influences the average performance.}
elements.sent-2411-271: Our final mixes for \tuluthreesmalldpo and \tuluthreelargedpo are displayed in Table~\ref{tab:final_pref_mix .}
elements.sent-2411-272: In summary, our preference mixes come from different prompt sources, such as SFT data, WildChat and Persona IF.
elements.sent-2411-273: It includes prompts seen during SFT training but also new, unseen prompts.
elements.sent-2411-274: And lastly, it contains a mix of on and off-policy completions.

elements.paragraph-2411-015: Scaling the Number of Unique Prompts Improve Downstream DPO Performance.
elements.sent-2411-277: First, we investigate whether increasing the number of prompts will yield improvements in downstream DPO performance.
elements.sent-2411-278: To do so, we measure the downstream DPO model performance at different sizes of a fixed amount of preferences with unique prompts.
elements.sent-2411-279: This suggests that dataset scaling is important to achieve improvements in downstream model performance: our final preference mixes (Table \ref{tab:final_pref_mix ) contain more than 270k data points for the 8b model and more than 330k instances for the 70b model - which is considerably bigger than many available preference datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-280: We also explore whether duplicating prompts (but with different responses) is a viable approach to scaling the size of a preference dataset and whether it will lead to gains in downstream DPO performance.
elements.sent-2411-281: To do so, we expanded the Ultrafeedback dataset, which originally had four responses for each prompt, by creating different pair combinations of responses.
elements.sent-2411-282: This expansion will naturally cause duplicated prompts, but with different chosen and rejected pairs sampled from the four responses in UltraFeedback, leading to preference datasets with 64k-, 180k-, and 383k instances.
elements.sent-2411-283: We also observe a slight performance degradation on DROP, GSM8k, and AlpacaEval as the number of duplicated prompts increase.
elements.sent-2411-284: This suggests that scaling via prompt duplication does not necessarily yield into significant gains in downstream DPO performance, and investing in the collection of unique prompts and proper mixing is more important for downstream evaluations.
elements.paragraph-2411-016: Unused Prompts Lead to Higher Performance vs. Reusing Prompts From SFT Mix.
elements.sent-2411-285: We then compare including new prompts and re-using prompts from the SFT stage on their effect on downstream DPO performance.
elements.sent-2411-286: To do so, we sampled 100k prompts from the SFT dataset mix that were used during training (as shown in \autoref{tab:sft_summary ) and compare it against prompts from the same open datasets (e.g., OpenAssistant, SciRIFF, Aya, Persona, WildChat, etc.) we subsampled from but left unused during SFT.}
elements.sent-2411-287: This suggests that the presence of new prompts can help improve downstream DPO performance.
elements.sent-2411-288: As seen in our best mix, combining unused and reused prompts seems to lead to the best result.
elements.paragraph-2411-017: On-policy Data Improves Downstream DPO Performance.
elements.sent-2411-289: We investigate whether the inclusion of on-policy data, i.e., text generations from the SFT model that will be used as the base model for preference finetuning, improves downstream model performance.
elements.sent-2411-290: Given the same set of prompts sourced from the SFT mix in \autoref{sec:sft , we generate preferences from off-policy models and compared it to a mix that is strictly on-policy (i.e., one of the response is always from the Initial 8B SFT model, and the other response is from the off-policy models).}
elements.sent-2411-291: We also compare it on a combination of both on-policy and off-policy data: we sample instances from the strict on-policy dataset and add it to the off-policy dataset so that the responses from each model is distributed equally.
elements.paragraph-2411-018: Performance Across LLM Judges are Similar, with GPT-4o Leading Slightly Ahead.
elements.sent-2411-292: In order to determine which judge to use for obtaining preference annotations, we test several commercial and open-source LLM judges such as GPT-4 (GPT-4-turbo-2024-04-09, GPT-4o-2024-08-06, gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18) and Llama 3.1 (70B and 405B) on the same set of 10k randomly-sampled Ultrafeedback prompts and responses.
elements.sent-2411-293: In general, GPT-4o, Llama3.1 405B, and GPT-4 turbo perform similarly across all benchmarks, with GPT-4o leading slightly ahead on the aggregated average performance as shown in \autoref{tab:pref_ablation_best_judge .}
elements.sent-2411-294: In the synthetic preference pipeline for \modelname{ ~3, we opted for GPT-4o-2024-08-06 due to its ease-of-use, cheaper cost per request, and batch inference speed via OpenAI's Batch API.\footnote{\url{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/batch.}}}

elements.sent-2411-302: We created preference data targeted to improve a model's precise instruction following skills.
elements.sent-2411-303: Persona IF: We take a subset of our collected instruction following SFT dataset, If-Persona-Sft and convert it into a preference dataset.
elements.sent-2411-304: Each example in If-Persona-Sft dataset contains a (prompt, constraints, response) tuple.
elements.sent-2411-305: We start by rewriting each prompt in the subset to relax one of the given constraints.
elements.sent-2411-306: More specifically, we prompt GPT-4o to generate rewrites such that the new response to the modified prompt is no longer a valid response for the original prompt (does not satisfy all the constraints).
elements.sent-2411-307: We then use the response to the new modified prompt as the rejected response, and create (chosen, rejected) pairs to form our If-Persona-Pref dataset containing close to 20K examples.
elements.sent-2411-308: IF-augmented: We randomly sample instructions from the \modelname~2 SFT mix and combine them with constraints from the taxonomy in \citet{zhou2023instructionfollowingevaluationlargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-309: The chosen and rejected completions are obtained through the synthetic pipeline in \S\ref{sec:pref_pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-310: WildChat IF: We sample instructions from WildChat \citep{zhao2024wildchat which contain constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-311: For this purpose we asked GPT-4 to extract whether or not a prompt includes a constraint.
elements.sent-2411-312: For IF-augmented, we run two analyses.
elements.sent-2411-313: We generate an additional set of more than 66k instances and we then run the chosen completions through constraint verifier functions, and only add those instances to the final set which actually fulfilled the constraint(s).
elements.sent-2411-314: This leaves us with a cleaned set of about 26k preferences, which we call IF-augmented-verified.
elements.sent-2411-315: In Figure \ref{fig:IF_ablations we show that the IF-persona preferences significantly improve IFEval scores beyond the baseline mix, while minimally harming average performance.}
elements.sent-2411-316: The IF-augmented-verified dataset improves IFEval performance only by 1 point, while also slightly harming the average performance.
elements.sent-2411-317: Combining IF-persona with IF-augmented-verified leads to the best IFEval performance, but to a slightly lower average.
elements.sent-2411-318: We therefore choose to include IF-augmented (not verified) and Persona IF in the final 8B DPO mix, which leads to both a satisfiying average and IFEval score.

elements.sent-2411-335: Also, the linear head to output reward scalars is initialized with weights according to $\mathcal{N (0, 1/\sqrt{(d_{\text{model + 1) )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: We use the same prompts in the DPO preference mix to make a controlled comparison between DPO and PPO.

elements.sent-2411-338: Evaluating RM's performance can be tricky because strong RM performance on RM-specific benchmarks does not necessarily translate to better downstream performance for PPO~\citep{ivison2024unpacking, chen2024accuracy .}
elements.sent-2411-339: Furthermore, iterating with RM and PPO is more expensive then iterating with DPO, so we decided to do most of our preference tuning experiments via DPO.

elements.sent-2411-343: PPO Gets Similar Average Scores with DPO in this Non-Tuned Setup Overall, we found that PPO could reach a comparable level of performance to DPO (albeit slightly lower) in this controlled setup.
elements.sent-2411-344: PPO is More Computationally Expensive The PPO runtime is roughly 28 hours using two nodes, whereas the DPO runtime is about 4 hours using a single node.
elements.sent-2411-345: If we use more computational budget or do more tuning, it is entirely possible that we can push up the PPO's performance even higher.
elements.sent-2411-346: However, given limited resources and the subtlety in RM evaluation, using DPO for preference tuning seems more economical.
elements.sent-2411-347: We decide to use PPO primarily for RLVR, to be introduced in Section~\ref{sec:rlvr .}
elements.sent-2411-348: To run the 70B DPO training, we found it useful to implement two key optimizations for reducing the GPU footprint of DPO training:.
elements.sent-2411-349: Caching DPO Log Probs To reduce GPU memory usage, we pre-compute and cache log probabilities across the dataset using the initial model, rather than keeping a reference DPO model in memory during training like the canonical implementation~\citep{vonwerra2022trl,rafailov2024direct .}
elements.sent-2411-350: This optimization eliminates the need to allocate GPU memory for the reference model.
elements.sent-2411-351: Separate Forward Passes for Chosen and Rejected Sequences The canonical DPO implementation~\citep{vonwerra2022trl,rafailov2024direct also concatenates the chosen and rejected sequences during the forward pass, effectively doubling the batch size and increasing GPU memory requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-352: To save GPU memory, we simply perform the forward passes separately on the chosen and rejected.

PPO can get similar (though slightly lower) average scores as DPO.} \label{fig:the-dpo-vs-ppo-chart} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_images/post-training/dpo_cache_memory.png} \caption{The peak GPU memory allocated can be reduced by caching the reference policy's logprobs on the preference dataset and doing forward passes separately for the chosen and rejected pairs.} \label{fig:dpo_cache_memory} \end{minipage}

elements.sent-2411-354: As expected, the model uses less GPU memory when using the two techniques on an 8xH100, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:dpo_cache_memory .}

elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.55\linewidth]{figures_images/rlvrv4.pdf} \caption{An overview of how Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) works. We sample completions from a policy model given a set of prompts, and verify their correctness using a deterministic function. If the answer is verifiably correct, we provide reward of $\alpha$, otherwise 0. We then train against this reward using PPO. } \label{fig:rlvr-system}
elements.sent-2411-355: In \modelname3, we introduce Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), a novel method for training language models on tasks with verifiable outcomes such as mathematical problem-solving and instruction following.
elements.sent-2411-356: RLVR leverages the existing RLHF objective but replaces the reward model with a verification function, as shown conceptually in Figure~\ref{fig:rlvr-system .}
elements.sent-2411-357: When applied to domains with verifiable answers, such as mathematics and verifiable instruction following tasks~\citep{zhou2023instructionfollowingevaluationlargelanguage , RLVR demonstrates targeted improvements on benchmarks like GSM8K while maintaining performance across other tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-358: RLVR can be seen as a simplified form of existing approaches for bootstrapping LM reasoning~\citep{zelikman2022star, Zelikman2024QuietSTaRLM, hoffman2023training or a simpler form of RL with execution feedback~\citep{gehring2024rlefgroundingcodellms , in which we simply use answer matching or constraint verification as a binary signal to train the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-359: While this has been done for improving math skills alone in prior work~\citep{VinePPO , we further extend RLVR to cover multiple evaluations and test how it can improve overall model performance, integrating it as a component of a generalist training pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-360: RLVR is based on a simple principle, common in RL literature, applied to language models: the policy only receives a reward when its generated responses are verifiably correct.
elements.sent-2411-361: More specifically, RLVR optimizes the following objective:\footnote{This is very similar to the standard KL-constrained RLHF objective, shown in Eq.~\ref{eq:rlhf-objective , with a reward function instead of a learned reward model. \begin{equation}\label{eq:rlvr-objective} \max_{\pi_\theta} \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi_\theta(x)} \left[R_\text{RLVR}(x, y)\right] = \left[ v(x,y) - \beta \text{KL}[\pi_\theta(y|x) \| \pi_\text{ref} (y|x)] \right]\end{equation} where $v$ is the verifiable reward function.}}
elements.sent-2411-362: We set $\alpha = 10$ based on pilot experiments and did not tune it further.
elements.sent-2411-363: Creating data for RLVR entails obtaining prompts with an accompanying binary verifier (i.e., constructing a set of inputs $x$ with accompanying verifier functions $v$).
elements.sent-2411-364: We focus on two domains (mathematics, exact instruction following) and three evaluations (GSM8K, MATH, IFEval) with relatively straightfoward methods for verification, and leave more complex verifiers to future work.\footnote{For example, recent work has found success in using code execution feedback to train models with RL~\citep{gehring2024rlefgroundingcodellms, xudpoppo .}}
elements.sent-2411-365: In practice, the answer extraction and verification method is domain-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-366: We use three sources of training prompts and verifiers:.
elements.sent-2411-367: We use the GSM8k training set.
elements.sent-2411-368: We augment each sample with the standard 8-shot prompt using during evaluation to encourage the model to use chain-of-thought, and then extract the final number produced and compare to the ground-truth label to determine correctness.
elements.sent-2411-369: We use the MATH training set.
elements.sent-2411-370: Similar to GSM8k, we augment each sample with the standard 3-shot CoT prompt used to encourage the model to generate chains of thought during evaluation, and then extract the answer and determine correctness following the `flex' MATH evaluation logic.
elements.sent-2411-371: We randomly sample instructions from the \modelname~2 SFT mix and combine them with constraints from the taxonomy in \citet{zhou2023instructionfollowingevaluationlargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-372: We have a verification function for each of the constraint templates that is able to verify whether a completion satisfies a constraint.
elements.sent-2411-373: Given these prompts and verification functions, we then train the models via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)~\citep{schulman2017proximal on these verifiable rewards.}
elements.sent-2411-374: We combine all prompts together, results in a mixture of roughly 30,000 prompts with ground truth labels.
elements.sent-2411-375: We summarize our verifiable prompt mixture in Table~\ref{tab:verifiable_prompt_summary .}
elements.sent-2411-376: RL and RLHF have many subtle implementation details that can significantly impact training stability~\citep{Engstrom2020Implementation,shengyi2022the37implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-377: As we use PPO to train our models against our verifiers, we adapt these effective implementation details from \citet{huang2024thenimplementationdetails :.}
elements.sent-2411-378: Initialize the Value model from a General RM.
elements.sent-2411-379: We initialize the value model from the reward model for the RLVR setup (following the standard setup in \citet{ziegler2019fine,ouyang2022training ).}
elements.sent-2411-380: We set the dropout probability to be 0 during RM and RL training (similar to \citet{ziegler2019fine ).}
elements.sent-2411-381: This ensures the token log probabilities can be computed deterministically during the forward passes of the policy model and reference model, allowing a more accurate estimation of the KL penalty.
elements.sent-2411-382: Furthermore, PPO calculates the token log probabilities in two phases: during the rollout phase and the learning phase.
elements.sent-2411-383: It is important to ensure the token log probabilities match up during these two phases: they produce a probability ratio of 1 during the first PPO epoch, so PPO can clip the ratio and apply the surrogate objective properly.
elements.sent-2411-384: If the log probabilities differ drastically due to dropout, all ratios could potentially be clipped, resulting in zero gradient.
elements.sent-2411-385: Train with the SFT Dataset and Shuffle Between Epochs.
elements.sent-2411-386: As pointed out in in \citet{huang2024thenimplementationdetails , PPO can train for more episodes than the total available prompts, effectively training for multiple epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-387: In our RLVR ablation experiments, we train for roughly $100000 / 7473 \approx 13$ epochs.
elements.sent-2411-388: We shuffle the prompts in between epochs.
elements.sent-2411-389: For our final runs, we examine model checkpoints every 40-100 steps and choose the best checkpoint on our development evaluation set.
elements.sent-2411-391: During training, PPO typically sample a fixed amount of maximum tokens.
elements.sent-2411-392: If the sampled response does not end with an EOS token, we give a -10 penalty to encourage the model to always complete its responses.
elements.sent-2411-394: Like done in standard PPO implementation details literature~\citep{Engstrom2020Implementation,shengyi2022the37implementation,huang2024thenimplementationdetails , we normalize the advantages by subtracting its mean followed by dividing its standard deviation.}

elements.sent-2411-410: Below are our main findings: RLVR Can Improve Performance in Targeted Domains.
elements.sent-2411-411: Figure~\ref{fig:the-rl-chart shows training with RLVR results in improved test performance for all three settings.}
elements.sent-2411-412: In all cases, we achieve models that outperform the initial model in that particular evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-413: We also see that the verifiable rewards (i.e., correctness on the train set) improves consistently for all three settings.
elements.sent-2411-414: Interestingly, in GSM8K and MATH, we found that incurring more KL budget does not necessarily lead to improvements in verifiable rewards.
elements.sent-2411-415: Initializing RLVR's Value Function from a General RM Works Best.
elements.sent-2411-416: Figure~\ref{fig:x32_RLVR_KL shows that initializing the value from a general RM obtains the highest GSM8K test score and also higher average scores.}

elements.sent-2411-432: Out PPO infrastructure leverages Zero Stage 3~\citep{rajbhandari2020zero to fit the models and applicable optimizer states into the memory.}
elements.sent-2411-433: In RLVR, we have 3 models: the policy model, the reference policy model, and the value model.
elements.sent-2411-434: The policy and value models need to be trained, but the reference policy model only performs inference.
elements.sent-2411-435: Often inference time is a bottleneck in RLHF infrastructure, so we allocate dedicated GPUs to do inference like done in OpenRLHF~\citep{hu2024openrlhf .}
elements.sent-2411-436: In particular, we use Ray~\citep{moritz2018ray to allocate dedicated GPUs to run PagedAttention via vLLM~\citep{kwon2023efficient .}}
elements.sent-2411-437: PagedAttention helps reduce GPU memory fragmentation and redundant duplication leveraging virtual memory and paging techniques.
elements.sent-2411-438: As a result, it helps our PPO infrastructure run LLM inference using a much larger batch size and speed up inference.
elements.sent-2411-439: This setup allowed us to scale PPO policy training to the 70B scale.. Asynchronous RL Training.
elements.sent-2411-440: Furthermore, our PPO setup uses asynchronous RL training to improve training efficiency~\citep{noukhovitch2024asynchronousrlhffasterefficient .}
elements.sent-2411-441: The existing PPO frameworks~\citep{hu2024openrlhf,shen2024nemoaligner are typically synchronous: the inference GPUs would first collect policy rollout data, and then the learner GPUs would train on that rollout data.}
elements.sent-2411-442: This setup would utilize GPU relatively well under a typical setup with Atari~\citep{mnih2015human .}
elements.sent-2411-443: However, under the RLHF setup, inference computation typically requires different optimizations (e.g., PagedAttention), thus requiring different GPU memory allocation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-444: As a result, synchronous RL training means inference GPUs could be idling while the learner GPUs run, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-445: An alternative implementation is to use the same set of GPUs for training and inference, but it could incur additional overhead such as an additional copy of the policy parameters and compiling inference engine in the training GPUs~\citep{shen2024nemoaligner .}
elements.sent-2411-446: Our setup allocates GPUs specifically for inference and training, alleviating the need to compile an inference engine and save a copy of the policy parameters in the training GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-447: Furthermore, we run the inference computation concurrently with the training computation, thus reducing the GPU idle time.
elements.sent-2411-448: However, asynchronous RL training can introduce stale data, in cases where the inference can generate data much faster than training consumes~\citep{espeholt2018impala , which can introduce reproducibility problems~\citep{huang2023cleanba .}}
elements.sent-2411-449: To help make training more reproducible, our setup always trains the policy using the second latest inference data~\citep {huang2023cleanba,noukhovitch2024asynchronousrlhffasterefficient .}

elements.sent-2411-450: Based on the above results, we ran our final RLVR runs using the combined verifiable prompt set, and used the best DPO models from the prior section as starting points.
elements.sent-2411-451: Specifically, at 8B scale, we tested the best overall DPO model and the best model with IFEval persona data mixed in during training, and at 70B scale we use the best overall DPO model.
elements.sent-2411-452: For hyperparameters, for 8B models, we used the hyperparameters from Table~\ref{tab:hypers_rl_vs_dpo , but tested higher KL penalty coefficients (up to 0.15) based on previous 8B RL development runs.}
elements.sent-2411-453: For 70B models, we used the hyperparameters from Table~\ref{tab:hypers_rl_vs_dpo , but with a 1 $\times$ 10\textsuperscript{-7 learning rate, 0.1 warmup ratio, 2048 response length, 400,000 episodes, 640 effective batch size, and $\beta=0.7$ based on previous 70B RL development runs.}}
elements.sent-2411-454: We initialize our value model from a reward model trained on the same dataset as the best DPO model (the \modelname~3 8B preference mixture) starting from \modelname~3 SFT, using the same hyperparameters as in our ablation experiments (Table~\ref{tab:hypers_rl_vs_dpo_rm ).}
elements.sent-2411-455: We evaluated our models every 100 training steps (40 for 70B), and picked as our final 8B model the checkpoints with best overall performance on MATH and IFEval.
elements.sent-2411-456: We show the logs from RLVR training in Figure~\ref{fig:final_rlvr_run , and compare the final performance against their DPO starting points and Llama 3.1 in Table~\ref{tab:rlvr_final_results .}}
elements.sent-2411-457: RLVR results in non-trivial improvements at the 8B scale, improving all three of MATH, GSM8k, and IFEval.
elements.sent-2411-458: In fact, we observed that some 8B runs were able to achieve GSM8k scores of up to 89.4\% and IFEval scores of up to 84.8\% (although such models tended to perform worse in other metrics, dragging down their overall average).
elements.sent-2411-459: At the 70B scale, we observe more modest improvements in IFEval and MATH, and no improvement in GSM8k, likely due to the fact that it is already close to saturation (93.5\%).
elements.sent-2411-460: Surprisingly, we find that our 70B run displays extremely low KL divergence, remaining well below 1 over the duration of run, probably due to the lower learning rate\footnote{We had attempted using a higher learning rate during the initial exploration but found that KL could explode initially and cause a non-trivial drop in average scores.. .}
